coredata.dc:title: Turn-off fluorescence of nitrogen and sulfur carbon quantum dots as effective fluorescent probes for determination of imatinib. Application to biological fluids
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Abd Elhaleem, Shymaa M.
Elsebaei, F.
Shalan, Sh.
Belal, F.
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen and sulfur carbon quantum dots(N,S-CQDs) as effective fluorescent nanoprobes were synthesized through one-step-hydrothermal method using thiosemicarbazide (as nitrogen and sulfur source) and citric acid (as carbon source). The highly fluorescent N,S-CQDs were subjected to various characterization techniques. The fluorescence of the synthesized N,S-CQDs is characterized by maximum fluorescence emission at 415 nm after excitation at 345 nm and a high quantum yield of 0.58. The native N,S-CQDs fluorescence is quantitatively quenched upon addition of imatinib (IMA), so they are used for its spectrofluorimetric determination in its pharmaceutical formulations and biological fluids. Under optimal conditions, N,S-CQDs exhibited a “turn-off” fluorescence response to IMA over the range of 1.0 to 15.0 µg/mL with a limit of quantification of 0.42 µg/mL and a lower detection limit of 0.14 µg/mL. Stern-Volmer equation was used to study the mechanism of quenching and it was found to occur through static quenching mechanism. The method was extended to the in-vitro determination of the drug in spiked human urine and plasma samples and the percent recoveries were satisfactory.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen and sulfur-carbon quantum dots
Imatinib
Static quenching
Biological fluids

Analysis of IMA in dosage form:
Determination of IMA in spiked human plasma:
Determination of IMA in spiked human urine:
Analysis of IMA in its commercial capsules:
Determination of IMA in human plasma:
Determination of IMA in human urine:

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is funded by Mansoura University (project code number: Mu-phra-21–12)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N,S-CQDs(a, a‘).
Fluorescence emission spectra of N,S-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths.
The typical TEM image of the N,S-CQDs.

Fluorescence emission spectra of the N,S-CQDs in aqueous solution upon addition of various concentrations of IMA (from top to bottom: 0 μg/mL, 1.0 μg/mL, 3.0 μg/mL, 5.0 μg/mL, 7.0 μg/mL, 9.0 μg/mL, 11.0 μg/mL, 15.0 μg/mL).
A co-plot showing the great overlap between UV–Vis absorption spectrum of IMA [5.0 μg/mL] (a) and fluorescence excitation spectrum of the N,S-CQDs(b).

Suppressed efficiency of observed and corrected fluorescence of N,S-CQDs after addition of different concentrations of IMA (1.0 μg/mL, 3.0 μg/mL, 5.0 μg/mL, 7.0 μg/mL, 9.0 μg/mL, 11.0 μg/mL, 15.0 μg/mL).
Stern-Volmer plot betweenF0/Fand concentration of IMA (1.0 μg/mL, 3.0 μg/mL, 5.0 μg/mL, 7.0 μg/mL, 9.0 μg/mL, 11.0 μg/mL, 15.0 μg/mL).
General procedure for synthesis and application of N,S-CQDs.
Analytical performance data for the determination of IMA by the proposed method.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Where; Sa = standard deviation of the intercept of the calibration curve.
b = slope of the calibration curve.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Application of the proposed method for the determination of IMA in raw material and pharmaceutical preparations.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: N.B. a Mean of three determinations.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The values between parentheses are the tabulated t and F values at P = 0.05.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Precision data for the determination of IMA by the proposed method.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: N. B. Each result is the average of three separate determinations

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Application of the proposed fluorescence sensor for IMA determination in spiked human plasma and urine.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Each result is the average of three separate determinations.

head.ce:title: Turn-off fluorescence of nitrogen and sulfur carbon quantum dots as effective fluorescent probes for determination of imatinib. Application to biological fluids

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, 35516, Mansoura, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, 35516, Mansoura, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen and sulfur carbon quantum dots(N,S-CQDs) as effective fluorescent nanoprobes were synthesized through one-step-hydrothermal method using thiosemicarbazide (as nitrogen and sulfur source) and citric acid (as carbon source). The highly fluorescent N,S-CQDs were subjected to various characterization techniques. The fluorescence of the synthesized N,S-CQDs is characterized by maximum fluorescence emission at 415 nm after excitation at 345 nm and a high quantum yield of 0.58. The native N,S-CQDs fluorescence is quantitatively quenched upon addition of imatinib (IMA), so they are used for its spectrofluorimetric determination in its pharmaceutical formulations and biological fluids. Under optimal conditions, N,S-CQDs exhibited a “turn-off” fluorescence response to IMA over the range of 1.0 to 15.0 µg/mL with a limit of quantification of 0.42 µg/mL and a lower detection limit of 0.14 µg/mL. Stern-Volmer equation was used to study the mechanism of quenching and it was found to occur through static quenching mechanism. The method was extended to the in-vitro determination of the drug in spiked human urine and plasma samples and the percent recoveries were satisfactory.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, fluorescentcarbon quantum dots(CQDs), as a novel member of nanomaterials with size-dependent optical and electronic properties, have attracted great attention due to their remarkable performance. The CQDs offer many fascinating features, includingsize- and excitation-dependent photoluminescence, high photo-stability, robust chemical inertness, easy preparation, eco-friendliness, and resistance to metabolic degradation in bio-applications. These properties result from the quantum confinement impact or conjugated π-domains. CQDs are preferable to traditional semi-conductorquantum dotsin the following aspects of biocompatibility, elevated fluorescent stability, and remarkable eco-friendly [1]. Several methods have been reported for CQDs preparation, such as chemical oxidation [2–4], electrochemical oxidation [5,6],hydrothermal cutting strategies [7]and carbonizing organics methods [8].However, most of these developed methods are not preferred because of expensive equipments, difficult procedures, and low yields. In this study, one-step hydrothermal treatment was used to obtain low-cost and high-yield CQDs with strong fluorescence emission.

Heteroatom doping of CQD is a remarkable and significant manner that occurs through introducing novel functional groups or creating reactions between carbon and doping atoms to tune the fluorescence feature. Distinct features of heteroatom doping, mainly Nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) and Sulfur-doped CQDs (S-CQDs) have been reported by many researchers [9].

In this study, both citric acid (CA) and thiosemicarbazide (TSC) were utilized as precursors to prepare N,S-CQDs via one-pot-hydrothermal technique, where CA serves as a carbon source and TSC serves as N and S source.

Imatinib mesylate [IMA] (Fig. 1 ) is α-(4-Methyl-1-piperazinyl)-3ʹ-{[4-(3-pyridyl)-2-pyrimidinyl]amino}- p-tolu-p-toluidide methanesulfonate [10]. It is an oral medication with antineoplastic activity used for the treatment of many cancers includingchronic myeloid leukemia (CML), acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) and gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GIST). It is atyrosinekinase inhibitor thatprevents tumor growth through decreasing the activity of proteins of cancer cells that control cell division, growth and survival [11].

Many reports have been published for IMA evaluation and investigation ,viz, spectrophotometry [12,13], spectrofluorimetry [14–16], HPLC [17–22], TLC [23,24], capillary electrophoresis [25–31]and electrochemistry [32–37].

Inner filter effect (IFE) has been exhibited as an influential tool for changing the signals of absorption into fluorescence. Absorption signals are produced because of absorption of the excitation and/or emission of light in the detection system [38].

The quenching of fluorescent N,S-CQDs after IMA addition through IFE and Stern –Volmer interaction has been tested. Further, the pH, incubation time, and temperature impact on fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDs were discussed.

Through comparing the developed method with other reported methods for determination of IMA using fluorescent quantum dots, it was found that, in the method involving chemical oxidation of starch , the working concentration range of IMA was 10–400 μg mL−1 [39], and that prepared through biosynthesis using microalgae cells, IMA linearity rangewas 10.43– 417.30 μg mL−1 [38]. The proposed method is far more sensitive compared with those reported methods.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Imatinib mesylate standard of 99.90% purity with (Batch No. Y0001695) was obtained from LC Laboratories (Woburn, MA, USA)
Glivec® Capsules, (Serial No. 10899729436196) containing 100 mg IMA/ capsule, a product of NOVARTIS Pharmaceutical Co., Cairo, Egypt; purchased from local Pharmacy.
Boric acid, acetic acid 96%, phosphoric acid, hydrochloric acid, and sodium hydroxide were obtained from Sigma - Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany).
Citric acid and thiosemicarbazide were bought from Sigma-Aldrich (USA).
Human plasma samples were attained from Mansoura University Hospitals [Mansoura, Egypt] and kept frozen until use.
Urine samples were collected from healthy male volunteers (30–40 years old).
Through the study, ultra-pure water was used, and all chemicals were of Analytical Grade.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy was performed using (Shimadzu UV–Visible 1601 spectrophotometer, Japan) with 1 cm quartz cell. Allphotoluminescence (PL) measurementswere carried out using Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer equipped withXenon flash lamp (800 V) at 5 nm slit width. Magnetic stirrer (Daihan Scientific CO., South Korea) and centrifuge (model 2-16P, SIGMA, Germany).Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) was conducted on a FT-IR spectrophotometer (IS10 Nicolet, USA). All measurements were performed in the range of 4000–400 cm−1 and recorded as 32 scans at 8 cm−1 resolution.Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on JEOL, JSM −2100 (Tokyo, Japan). The sample was examined at 200 KV after being loaded on a 200-mesh carbon-coated Cu-grid.

N,S-CQDs were successfully synthesized from compounds rich with carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur by one-step-hydrothermal method. Typically, 0.68 g of thiosemicarbazide (7.50 mmol) and 0.52 g of citric acid (2.50 mmol) were transferred into a small flask, dissolved in 20 mL of ultra-pure water then refluxed for 8 h at 180 °C to obtain an orange solution (1.0 g/L) then filtered and stored in a refrigeratoruntil used [40]. The prepared dots could be applied to ‘turn-off’ determination of IMAwith remarkable selectivity and sensitivity. The procedure of N,S-CQDs synthesis is illustrated in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of buffer and stock solutions:
ce:section.ce:para: Britton Robinson buffers (BRB) covering pH range of 2.2–12 were used during the study. They were prepared by mixing 0.04 M phosphoric acid, 0.04 M boric acid, 0.04 M acetic acid, then pH was adjusted using 0.2 M sodium hydroxide.

In order to obtain a stock solution of IMA with a concentration of (100.0 µg/mL), 0.01 g of IMA mesylate was dissolved in 100 mL of ultra-pure water with the aid of 1 mL of 0.1 M HCl to allow complete solubility of IMA powder. The stock solution was further diluted to obtain different concentrations as appropriate.
Ultra-pure water was used during preparation of N,S-CQDs solution (1.0 g/L), and 10 mL of the stock was transferred to 100 mL measuring flask then completed to volume with ultra-pure water.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the dots was determined through an established procedure according to Equation (1) [41]:
(1)
ϕ
N , S - C Q D s
=
ϕ
QS
×
[
F
N , S - C Q D s
F
QS
]
×
[
A
QS
A
N , S - C Q D s
]
×
[
η
N , S - C Q D s
η
QS
]
2
Where,

ϕ
N,S-CQDs
represents the quantum yield of N,S-CQDs.

ϕ
Q
S
represents the quantum yield of QS.
F refers to the measured integrated fluorescence emission intensity.
A refers to the absorbance value of the solution.
η refers to the refractive index of the solvent (distilled water).

QS subscript represents standard quinine sulfate in 0.1 M sulfuric acid that distinguishes with (QY: 0.54 at 350 nm). Absolute values can be determined using the reference sample which has a constant quantum yield value.
η
N , S - C Q D s
η
Q S
is equal to 1 in aqueous solutions.

Transfer aliquots from the standard solution of IMA corresponding to a concentration ranging from (1.0 to 15.0 µg/mL) into volumetric flasks (10 mL), add 0.1 mL of N,S-CQDs solution then complete to volume using ultra-pure water. Measurement of fluorescence intensity was performed at 415 nm after excitation at 345 nm against a reagent blank treated similarly. The calibration graph between relative fluorescence intensity (RFI) and IMA concentrations (µg/mL) was plotted, meanwhile the regression equation was derived.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of IMA in dosage form:
ce:section.ce:para: Ten capsules (Glivec® 100 mg IMA /capsule) were evacuated and weighed. Transfer an accurately weighed quantity of the powder corresponding to 10.0 mg of IMA into a 100 mL volumetric flask then dissolve in about 70 mL of water with the help of 1 mL of 0.1 M HCl. Apply sonication for half an hour, complete to the volume with water, then filter. Aliquots from the filtrate containing the drug within the working range were transferred into 10 mL volumetric flasks, then the procedure under section “2.6.1” was performed. The regression equation was utilized to calculate the nominal content of the capsules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of IMA in spiked human plasma:
ce:section.ce:para: Transfer aliquots (1 mL) of human plasma sample into screw-capped centrifugation tubes (10 mL) then spike with increasing aliquots of IMA standard solution (50.0 µg/mL), complete with methanol up to 5 mL then vortex-mix for 2 min. After centrifugation at 3600 rpm for 5 min, one milliliter aliquots of the supernatant were transferred into volumetric flasks (10 mL). Then 0.1 mL aliquots of N,S-CQDs solution were added, completed with ultra-pure water to the mark to obtain concentrations of IMA: 1.0, 1.5 and 2.5µg/mL.

The procedure described under section “2.6.1” was performed. A blank plasma sample was treated similarly. The percent recoveries of IMA were determined adopting the regression equation previously derived from data of RFI vs IMA concentration (µg/mL) in pure plasma.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of IMA in spiked human urine:
ce:section.ce:para: Into a series of screw-capped centrifugation tubes, 1 mL aliquots of drug-free human urine samples were transferred, then increasing volumes from the drug standard solution (50 µg/mL) were added, completed with methanol up to 5 mL, then vortex-mixed for 2 min. After centrifugation at 3600 rpm for 5 min, 1 mL aliquots were withdrawn from the supernatant of each tube and filtered through syringe filter then transferred to volumetric flask (10 mL). The procedure described under section“2.6.1” was carried out to get final drug concentrations of 1.0, 2.0 and 3.0 µg/mL. The fluorescence intensity readings were measured against a blank solution. The percent recoveries of IMA were calculated using the regression equation obtained from a plot between RFI and IMA concentration (µg/mL).

Several techniques were used for the characterization of the prepared N,S-CQDs. At first, UV–visible absorption spectra of TSC, CA, and N, S-CQDs were performed ( Supplementary material
Fig.S1 ). An aqueous solution of N,S-CQDs was measured spectrofluorimetricaly at 415 nm after excitation at 345 nm(Fig. 2 ).N,S-CQDs exhibit excitation-dependentphotoluminescencebehavior. The dots show different emission peaks after changing excitation wavelength starting from 320 up to 400 nm, as indicated in Fig. 3 . The maximum emission wavelength was found at 415 nm in accordance with the modification of the excitation wavelength to 345 nm.

TEM image was also used for further confirmation for the synthesized N,S-CQDs indicating mono-dispersed spherical nanoparticles within size range of 10
±
3 nm (Fig. 4 ). In addition, FT-IR analysis was done in order to illustrate the surface chemistry of N,S-CQDs [42]. Fig. 5 showed several functional groups on the surface of N,S-CQDs, such as NH, OH, CN, CO, CC, CS and CH groups with wavenumbers of 3381, 3180, 2065, 1711, 1624, 1232, 579 cm−1 ,respectively.

The native fluorescence of the prepared N,S-CQDs is intensely quenched upon addition of IMA, as presented in Scheme.1. Fig. 6 shows the emission fluorescence spectra of the prepared dots after addition of increasing concentrations of IMA. The quenching of fluorescence occurs according to several mechanisms including dynamic quenching, static quenching, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE).

In this study, there is an overlap between UV-absorbance spectrum of IMA and that of N,S-CQDs excitation spectrum (Fig. 7 ), so, IFE might occur. To investigate the mechanism of quenching, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDs after addition of increasing concentrations of the quencher (IMA) was corrected for possible IFE using equation #2:
(2)
F
corr
=
F
obs
X
10
A
ex
+
A
em
2
Where:
Fobs and Fcorr are the observed and the corrected fluorescence intensities after excluding IFE from Fobs, respectively.
Aex and Aem refer to the absorbance of the quencher (IMA) at the excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of N,S-CQDs, respectively.

The suppressed efficiency (%E) for both observed and corrected fluorescence were calculated according to Equation #3 :
(3)
% E =
1 -
F
F
O
x 100

The plot of %E of both observed and corrected fluorescence intensities of N,S-CQDs versus IMA concentrations in µg/mL (Fig. 8 ) indicated that, IFE didn’t have any role in the quenching of N,S-CQDs as IMA does not show any significant absorbance.

In order to define the mechanism that might be the cause of N,S-CQDs quenching, Stern Volmer Equation (4) was adopted.
(4)
F
o
F
= 1 +
K
sv
Q
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
Q
Where:
F0 and F refer to the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of the quencher, respectively.
Ksv refers to the Stern Volmer quenching constant.
[Q] refers to the quencher concentration.
Kq represents the quenching rate constant.

τ
0
is the average lifetime (10−8 s) [43].

Furthermore, the static quenching of interaction may be understood in terms of Stern-Volmer plot, as the values of Kq decreases with increasing the temperature 302, 313 and 323 oK ,as illustrated in Fig. 9 . It was concluded that, the quenching of fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDs in the presence of IMA was mainly due to static quenching mechanism [44].

In this method, Britton Robinson buffer covering pH range from (2.2 to 12) was used. It was found that, changing pH has no significant effect on the results, So buffers were excluded from the study, which made the proposed method simple.

The impact of incubation time upon the determination of IMA was also investigated carefully at various time intervals from 1 to 30 min. The results indicated that, the reaction was completed instantaneously and remained stable for more than 30 min., so incubation time was found to have no impact on the results.

The effect of the temperature on fluorescence was studied. The fluorescence intensity was measured at the optimum emission wavelength (415 nm) at 302, 313 and 323 oK. When the temperature increased from 302 to 323 oK, the fluorescence intensity continuously decreased. The obtained NSCDs demonstrate obviously temperature-dependent fluorescence behavior. Therefore, the study was performed at room temperature.

The proposed method was evaluated regarding ICH Q2R1 recommendations [45]. The fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDs solution decreases upon gradual increase of IMA concentration. Linearity of IMA was achieved with ideal correlation coefficient of 0.9999 within the concentration range from 1.0 to 15.0 µg/mL, as illustrated in Table 1 .

Limit of quantitation (LOQ) and limit of detection (LOD) values pointed out to remarkable sensitivity of the method, and hence its suitability to determine IMA in biological fluids, where LOQ is 0.42 µg/mL and LOD is 0.14 µg/mL (Table 1).
For exploring the accuracy of the method, it was necessary to compare the assay results for the raw material and the commercial capsules with those obtained using comparison method [13].

It was concluded that, there are no considerable differences between the proposed and comparison methods concerning the accuracy and precision using both Student t-test and Variance ratio F-test [46] (Table 2 ).

Intra-day and inter-day precisions of the proposed spectrofluorimetric method were examined by assaying three samples of increasing concentrations (5.0, 7.0, and 9.0 µg/mL) three times in the same day and on three consecutive days. Method precision is assured by the obtained minimal values of %RSD < 2% (Table 3 ) demonstrating perfect repeatability and reproducibility. Furthermore, the proposed method showed good robustness since no significant changes in the analytical response was observed upon minor changes in the experimental conditions, such as volume of N,S-CQDs (0.1 ± 0.01 mL).

Method specificity was examined via monitoring any interference that may result from the excipients and the additives of (Glivec® capsules). It was found that, there is no interference during the determination of IMA by the suggested method (Table 2).

Further, method selectivity was achieved through the analysis of IMA in spiked human plasma and urine without interference from plasma and urine components as revealed by the low % RSD values indicating that there are no significant differences between the proposed and comparison methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of IMA in its commercial capsules:
ce:section.ce:para: The developed spectrofluorimetric method was successfully used to determine IMA in its capsules. The reasonable recovery percent values indicated the ability of the method to estimate the drug in its dosage form without interference from excipients. An ideal harmonization is easily achieved between the proposed and the comparison method [13] after using both Student’s t-test and Variance Ratio F-test[46] for application of statistical analysis of the outcomes which indicated no significant differences concerning both accuracy and precision (Table 2 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of IMA in human plasma:
ce:section.ce:para: The previous data of the proposed method indicate the ability and the sensitivity of the method to detect and/or determine IMA in spiked human plasma as the previously reported maximum plasma concentration (Cmax) is 1.929 ± 0.479 µg/mL [47]. Upon applying statistical analysis of the data, the mean percentage recoveries and the SD of IMA in plasma samples are 96.09 ± 1.72 (Table 4 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of IMA in human urine:
ce:section.ce:para: The mean absolute bioavailability of IMA is 98%. Its major metabolism site is the liver, but around 13% is excreted in the urine [11,12]. The results reported in Table 4, were very satisfactory with acceptable recovery and ideal values of correlation coefficients indicating a linear behavior of the standard addition method. This method can be used for IMA detection in real human urine.

In this work, a simple hydrothermal synthetic treatment was used to obtain highly fluorescent N,S-CQDs. The combination of citric acid with thiosemicarbazide is economic and rich in several functional groups (COOH, OH, S and NH2), supplying both carbon, sulfur, and nitrogen sources in order to synthesize luminescent N,S-CQDs with high quantum yield. The prepared N,S-CQDs were found to be effective-fluorescent nano-sensor for sensitive and accurate determination of IMA over a linear range from 1.0 to 15.0 µg/mL with a lower detection limit of 0.14 µg/mL. Upon addition of IMA, the native N,S-CQDs fluorescence was progressively reduced as a result of the static quenching mechanism. The validated proposed method is eco-friendly, simple, low cost with excellent sensitivity for IMA detection both in pharmaceutical dosage forms and in biological fluids.
This work is funded by Mansoura University (project code number: Mu-phra-21–12)

Shymaa M. Abd Elhaleem: Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. F. Elsebaei: Validation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Sh. Shalan: Validation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. F. Belal: Conceptualization, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.120954.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pharmacokinetics and cellular uptake of imatinib and its main metabolite CGP74588
Cancer Chemother. Pharmacol.
